sections.title: Chairman's Chat
sections.content:
Martin Baxter reports

We had a lot to talk about at the last committee meeting.  Although the weather wasn’t conducive to flying, the Flyin and XDales still proved to be a success, with about £500 raised.  Although Eventbrite takes a fair slice of income in fees, we consider that prepayment is essential for weather related events. It remains to be seen how this year’s weather will influence numbers next year, but we have built up a financial reserve to see us through if attendance is poor.

Club finances are pretty much on track.  Last year we made a decent profit, which converts into a modest reduction in working capital once our contribution to the Flying (Savings) Fund is taken into account.  We plan for this trend to continue through 2025.  The Treasurer will publish his detailed report on the forum in due course, and you can come along to the AGM on 5 Dec if you want to know more.

As a result, we will be recommending to the membership that subscriptions continue at £25 (£22.50 for prompt payment) next year, with contributions to the Flying Fund remaining at 35%.

In addition, we’ll be proposing that travelling expenses for committee members come into line with HMRC approved rates.  In practical terms this means an increase from 40ppm to 45ppm next year.

Having researched the cost of a formal audit, we’re also going to propose that this be removed as a standing AGM agenda item, but that the accounts be made available to any member on request.

You may recall, from last summer, that a couple of pilots in the Dales round of the British Open Series (hang gliders) upset some farmers near Semer Water.  As a result, we withheld permission for the site to be used for competitions for one year.  There have been no complaints at Semer Water over the past 12 months, so we have now lifted the ban.

Flying conditions at Wether/Dodd Fell on Fri 13th Sep were excellent. Those of us flying locally were treated to a blow-by-blow account over the radio as the XC Hounds made their way to Hull.  At the committee meeting we discussed establishing a different radio frequency for pilots leaving the hill so that novices, perhaps involved in coaching, weren’t distracted; but concluded that it’s more important to have everybody on the same frequency in case of an incident.  My own personal solution is to do more XC next season!

sections.title: AGM Notice
sections.content:
Tam reports

DHPC Annual General Meeting

Thursday December 5th 2024. 7.30 for 8 pm start

Horse and Farrier, Bridge St, Otley. LS21 1BQ

All members of the Dales Hang Gliding and Paragliding Club are invited to attend the AGM, which will be followed by the presentation of the coveted club trophies and mugs for the 2024 season.

All proposals will be published in the November edition of Skywords and on the club forum. The Treasurer's report and reports from the other current committee members will be published on the forum by November 20th (members only). There is no opportunity to raise new issues for voting after 1st November, or on the night at the AGM. Voting on all proposals will be at the AGM in person only.

As well as setting membership subscriptions and contributions to the Flying Fund for the coming year, all committee posts are up for re-election, as per the constitution. Members are free to stand for any post - none of the incumbents will be offended if you wish to have a go at their role. Committee membership simply requires a little spare time and a willingness to put something back into the club. If you want to have a non-committal conversation about a role, then please contact the Chairman, Martin Baxter, at chairman@dhpc.org.uk.

sections.title: Start of the Winter Social Season - This Thursday 3rd Oct
sections.content:
Stef Sykes reports

With the Dales FlyIn behind us, we have our monthly series of social evenings to look forward to.

These take place on the first Thursday of the month between October and April. The first evening, then will be the 3rd of October at the Horse and Farrier pub in Otley, as ever.

For this first evening, we’ll start gently with a panel Q&A, as has been suggested by a few members over the summer. The panel will feature three experienced free-flyers who will introduce their areas of expertise and then the floor will be open to questions from members on any area of free-flying.

Please come armed with a useful (or amusing) question.

On November the 7th we have Richard Meek to look forward to. Richard plans to present his 6 best failures; those that produced the most useful lessons.

Then on December the 5th we have the AGM and annual prize and trophy award ceremony.

sections.title: Safety
sections.content: Clive Sury reports

subsections.title: Equipment Checks
subsections.content:
How often do you check your equipment? And do you know how often each item should be replaced, inspected and repaired?  Equipment failure has been a critical factor in incidents.  Indeed, there was a fatality in Oludeniz within the last year due to a faulty carabiner.  Let’s look at few of the key items:

  • Wing - should be formally inspected and serviced in accordance with the handbook (normally every 1 or 2 years).  Plus, before each flight the flyer should check it over for airworthiness
  • Reserve parachute.  Some manufacturers recommend repacking every six months, which is also the recommended interval by BHPA within their article on reserve parachutes - Parachute_Advice.pdf (bhpa.co.uk) - noting that if it becomes damp it should be aired immediately and repacked.  The recommended lifetime is 10 years with this subject to passing regular inspections.
  • Carabiners.  Should be replaced every 5 years or 500 hours, whichever sooner.  Again, to be replaced if warranted by inspection with coastal flying especially harmful to aluminium given the salt in the air.
  • Helmets.  To conform to EN966 (or similar) which is the specific certification for airsports helmets.  I cannot find specific guidance regarding a recommended lifetime use, however, as general guidance, motorcycle helmets are recommended to be replaced if dropped or subject to an accident, and changed every 5 years.  Earlier this year I witnessed a pilot with a BHPA 2002 sticker on his helmet and it raised a question on how effective it could be.
  • Footwear.  It’s been observed how some flyers wear walking shoes, training shoes and even nothing on their feet.  Whilst footwear is an individual’s choice, boots with ankle support are recommended given the demands of take-offs, landings and possibility of a PLF.
  • Hook knife.  Is this accessible for the flyer?  Some harnesses have these built in and it’s recommended for the flyer to be able to cut themselves free from the glider or reserve if required.
  • Harness.  Whilst it should be checked for general airworthiness, there have been numerous incidents involving reserves deploying accidentally which may have been avoided if checked that they were secure before flying.  In the past the sharp edges of carbon fibre seat boards have proved very effective at cutting through leg straps! Also, a good idea is to have a spare reserve bag/ handle pack so that you can continue flying if needing to use your reserve on a trip.
  • Radio.  Is it accessible for use in flight easily i.e., without having to release brake handles to physically use it?  This is a requirement for most SIV courses but on a general basis, effective communications whilst in flight is essential.  Don’t be the one to get in the way of the emergency helicopter that has come to rescue your friend.
  • Vario & Electronic Conspicuity devices.  You must stay out of controlled airspace and it makes sense to have a moving map if you are flying XC.  EC and tracking devices are not yet mandatory.  Many pilots fly with two devices, having one as a backup.  

The above is intended to prompt flyers in reviewing their equipment and clothing objectively, and update as they deem required.  Candidly when preparing for this article, it prompted me to fit a hook knife with my harness which I became re-familiar with having a built in secure strap to use as emergency support if landing in a tree.

The Club’s repack day for next year is booked for Saturday 18th January at Ilkley Grammar School.  This affords an opportunity to inspect one’s reserve and repack it under guidance.  Plus, to check or replace other items such as carabiners and connectors.  Booking details for this will be announced in good time.

Note:

Photograph courtesy of Skywalk Paragliders skywalk paragliders - INDIA | 2 - Markus Anders & Nicolas Manthos

subsections.content:
• Paraglider maillon/quick link connectors
  • Woody Valley hang gliding harnesses Tenax 3 with inside trim system and Tenax 4

Source details:  https://www.bhpa.co.uk/safety/advisory

sections.title: Coaching
sections.content:
Pete Logan, Chief Coach reports


Two new additions for the Pilot Rating in the Club this month. Congratulations go to Rhian Ebrey and Paul Rowntree who finished their tasks and passed the online exam.

Anyone wishing to get their Pilot Rating, if you have your tasks complete or very nearly complete then let your usual / local coach know or email coaching@dhpc.org.uk and we’ll get you organised for the exam. We will also look to do some exam revisions sessions as usual over winter.

You can try your skills with a mock exam here:

https://www.bhpa.co.uk/safety/mock_exams/ 


And here’s Marc from the BHPA providing some background on the need to get a Pilot rating…..

sections.title: Sites and News
sections.content:
Martin Baxter reports

Simon Browning has decided to quit paragliding and will therefore be standing down as Sites Officer (South) at the AGM.  He’s been sailing around Britain for most of the year so he hasn’t been able to dedicate much time to the job, but has promised a comprehensive handover, including the delivery of Christmas drinks to the farmers.  Managing our sites is a key role and we need to find a replacement.  Having done the job myself I can vouch for the fact that our farmers are a friendly bunch and most sites tick along quite nicely.  Helpful if you live in the vicinity (South of Kettlewell), but not essential. If you think that you might be able to help, or simply want to know more about it then please drop me a line: chairman@dhpc.org.uk

Postscript.  I’m delighted to announce that Stephen Craven has volunteered to stand.  Assuming that nobody wants to stand against him, that means that he’ll be unanimously elected at the AGM on 5 Dec; and that it’s safe for the rest of you to attend without the risk of getting accidentally elected if you scratch your nose at the wrong moment.

When I last flew at Stags Fell I noticed that the DHPC sign had broken, either succumbing to the elements or as a result of vandalism.  Tom leapt into action and ordered a replacement.  He also provided some plywood backing to make it a bit more resilient, but he didn’t have a saw, so I completed the project.  I recovered the old sign(s) and have included photos of them here for comparison.

It was a beautiful evening so, whilst I was there, I went in search of Cleasby’s Cave, with the aim of marking it as a hazard.  Not sure if I found the right one, but I marked it anyway.  It’s about 6’ (2m) deep and 10’ (3.5m) across.  I get the impression that, with it being Limestone, these things appear from time to time: watch your step.

sections.title: Sites Tour
sections.content:
Martin Baxter reports.

As part of the Flyin, I organised and ran a Sites Tour to introduce pilots to some of the sites around Hawes.  We visited Tailbridge, Stags Fell, Semer Water (NNW & SE), Wether Fell and Dodd Fell.  Sunny all day, but very windy.   If there is sufficient interest I’ll do another one, probably in February, when there is little chance of being distracted by some actual flying.

 
Photo: Rhian, Mike, Jordan, Martin, Dave, Dmitrjs

sections.title: Dales FlyIn 2024
sections.content:
Stef Sykes reports

Dales no-flying, more like!

Although the weather was glorious, with high pressure and warm sun finally helping define late summer, it was accompanied by strong winds that kept wings in their stuff bags.

It didn’t stop the fun, though. We had people coming from all over the country to enjoy site visits, climbing, cycling, walking, swimming-in-the-Ribble and axe throwing, among many other less exuberant activities.

Then the evenings brought alight the massive fire, roasted meats and delicious veggies on the barbecue and plenty of well-earned beer-soaked conviviality.

Ed called the X Dales for the Saturday and with the weather no different, many opted for the Hike-Only option for the Hike and Fly. A separate report in this month’s Skywords has been penned on this part of the weekend.

September is always a little tricky for flying in the Dales with Chris Kay pointing out that, from his research, only one in 20 days are flyable in the month, compared to one in 5 in May. However, this is the first Dales FlyIn where we’ve not been able to fly at all (well, excepting some very short hops by four pilots doing the X Dales).

With this fourth year of the event behind us, we certainly look forward to one next year and would be grateful for any feed-in for what else we can bring - if anything! - to the weekend.

Below - Adam Cox with a short flight at Stainforth Force into the River Ribble.

subsections.title: Beginner’s Fumble into Hike and Fly - Chapter 5
subsections.content:
Paul Rowntree reports

The forecast the weekend before was looking good, light winds and sun.  Route planning became ambitious – take off here, fly that ridge, grab a couple thermals to tag that cylinder, another thermal or two for that one, etc.  By mid-week, Friday was definitely going to be blown out.  By Friday night, there was just one forecast suggesting it could be flyable on Saturday morning.  The competition directors announced a rule change, there was no requirement to carry your glider.  In the hope that the majority of the forecasts were wrong and the one rogue one was correct, I went to sleep with my options open.  On waking with the sun, the trees in the camp site were still rocking back and forth, decision made, ditch the glider.  In carrying it, I might be tempted to try taking off at some stage knowing that the general weather conditions are not suitable.

The general chat amongst everyone was along the lines of “not to worry, let’s just go for a nice walk”.  Groups starting forming, but the competition was not cancelled, there were points to be collected.  People’s competitive edge could not be subdued.

The 2024 competition brought with it new rules.  You can use the train!  Get the first train of the day from Settle, if you get off at Horton in Ribblesdale, 25 points, Ribblehead – 50, Dent – 100, Garsdale – 150, Kirkby Stephen – 250.  There were 40 cylinders scattered around the Dales offering 20 to 100 points, the higher scorers further north, and something I only realised the night before, you can bag the points of multiple stations once you have left the train.  The further away you travel, the further it is to get back to base for 6:30pm, otherwise points are deducted, or scrapped if you are later than 8pm.  Or, you can accept a 25% point deduction if you use the train or a car to get back to base in time.

Of the 37 entrants, 18 were at the start line, a number with gliders.  I asked Tom Shuttleworth, who was planning on walking rather than running, what his plans were, to which he wasn’t set on any and was happy to join me.  My strategy came to me whilst sat on the throne – get off at Dent, walk up the eastern side to Kirkby Stephen through some high pointers and back down the other side to Garsdale.  Tom, sensibly adjusted it to starting at Garsdale and if we have time, finish at Dent.

As we reached the stations, tactics started to emerge.  Everyone resisted the gentle stroll back from Horton in Ribblesdale.  Half a dozen alighted at Ribblehead with Gary Crisp setting himself the three peaks challenge and a pack of five hunting down the nearer to base cylinders.

Dent platform didn’t gain any athletes.  We shared our planned route with Tom Hodgkin.  After a lot of map analysis, a couple of minutes before Garsdale he said, that’s a good idea, I’ll join you.  When we stood up to get off, there were mutterings of what are you doing, why not go to the next stop?  Tom H had a supporter for the day so was travelling ultra ultra light.  Pockets filled with reserves, no rucksack needed.  Tom S and I said goodbye to him just outside the station as he disappeared at speed, we spent the rest of the day following his spoor.  Jamie Moffat also started here but headed south towards base.

Kirkby Stephen platform collecting the remaining athletes who split east and west from the station.  Those with the big rucksacks were heading as quickly as they could to the east face of Wild Boar to see if the wind was light enough to take off.  For four brave pilots, it was, but they couldn’t stay in the lift otherwise they were into the cloud covering the tops.  It won’t go down as the smoothest flights of their careers, but they were able to traverse along the valley, albeit they had to stop to then climb the hill into the cloud to tag the cylinder.

No more flying stories for the day, plenty adopted the running strategy even those with gliders.  Charles Black was told it was too far to make it back to base from Kirkby Stephen on foot, so his mission was to prove that wrong.  On walking down the road from Tailbridge, Anna Aguila questioned why she sort out a winding footpath to walk up it.  I bored Tom S all day with my chat, although walking up to Wild Boar mid-afternoon, whilst the cloud had lifted, the wind was so strong all concentration was going into staying upright.

The pack on the Ribblehead back to base route managed to find a cream tea option en route.  Marching down the hill towards Garsdale, Tom and I had a decision to make, would we have time to make it to Dent?  We estimated we needed an hour from Garsdale station.  Given the full day of hiking in and out of bogs, blisters had joined the party and the legs weren’t telling us that running should suddenly form our strategy.  At best, it was looking like we had 50 mins for the trip.  At the start of the day, we passed the now closed Moorcock Inn, but it had a sign to the rear saying licenced café.  We felt the best thing to do with our spare 50 mins, was support a local business!  On arrival, we discovered Anna had the same view.  Having a leisurely drink and amble to Garsdale station, we suddenly heard the train, we were distracted, we were still in a competition.  A 200m sprint was required and we were fortunate the driver had some patience, you don’t get that service on the mainline!

With the scores added up, then recalculated, the winner accumulating 1,420 points, less 25% for not finishing in Lancliffe giving him 1,065 was Tom Hodgkin who covered a massive 69 km in the exercise.  Charles Black did prove that it was possible to go from the top back to Langcliffe without the need to fly and finishing in time to take second spot with 935 points.  Mike Cavanagh ran in part with a glider to claim the last spot on the podium with 810 points after deductions.  Tom and I claimed equal 4th with 720 points narrowly beating those who managed to fly.  Anna Aguilar took the top ladies spot with Rhian Ebrey and Susie Bilsborough tying for second.

A map showing the cylinders and routes each competitor took, their numbers at their starting station. Full results with competitor numbers at https://www.xcflight.com/x-dales-24-challenge/x-dales-24-results/ 

Could we have made it to Dent?  Quite possibly, but we would still have finished 4th.  We stopped for lunch, which we could have had on the hoof.  There were some trickier map reading sections in our route amongst the stone walled fields to tag the back of Kirkby Stephen station and back out, which we could have been sharper in working out the best route and there was a shortcut to Garsdale, that missed the Moorcock Inn, which would have given us a better chance if we’d opted to go for it.  More obviously, we could have run some of the downhills, but we set off to enjoy ourselves without breaking our bodies hoping for a top 10 position.  It is called hike (and fly)!

We certainly weren’t the only ones to be frustrated by stone walls, the walking back into Langcliffe pack were on the wrong side of one to tag a cylinder near the valley bottom.  If they had a different mapping app, they may have seen there was a way looping through a couple of other fields, but when you’re walking, going into another field or two off-course in the hope of a gap being in the wall isn’t an attractive option.

In travelling as a pair, Tom and I discussed different routes and short-cut options with our different apps which was useful, both of us would have taken different routes on our own.  Walking on a path is quicker as it is generally firmer under foot with little vegetation, there are gaps in the walls and navigation along them is easier.  Our total route was 44km.  On a good largely flat stoney bridleway stretch, we averaged 6kph for 3km.  Taking a shortcut up a steep hill that involved finding a gap in a wall dropped us down to 3kph for that km section, perhaps that didn’t pay off.  Surprisingly going downhill we were only travelling at 5kph, less than that when the footpath is boggy/winding through peat mounds.  Our route had few very steep sections, which makes a difference.

We didn’t all fly, but we learnt more about how we moved on the ground and improving our navigation techniques, which will certainly help for future events.  Thank you to Ed and Rob for running the event, the weather may not have played ball but we all enjoyed it.  Photograph credits to Sara Spillett and competitors.

sections.title: Sopot Part 2
sections.content:
John Edmonds reports

Continuing from last month's article…

So it has come around, a bit of ground handling with Joseph and a short, very short, flight from Windbank and now I’m back off to Sopot on a ‘Thermalling’ course.

Joseph and Tristan Hall are coming too, this should be fun!

The flight was a bit delayed, still our transfer was there to take us from the airport and it was much warmer than here in the UK, so far, so good.

We were taken to our accommodation which suited us all. Time to unpack and even wander down to the town for supplies for our self catering stay.

That was Saturday, Sunday free day my course started on Monday so we decided to go up Sopot Mountain. This was the only time where I knew more than Joseph or Tristan, I had done the ski lift up before and knew which way to go to the take off!

Joseph was being very concerned and wanted me to practise ground handling. To be honest the wind was off to the west and not ideal. So we messed about for a bit, I seemed to be doing ok but was getting a bit tired after 20 minutes or so we agreed that if the wind co-operated and I got things stable I’d go for a take off and fly down. I popped up the Geo 3 and it all looked good so off I went.

So far so good, I cleared the obstacles and started looking for lift and started 360’ing. I wasn’t going down and enjoying looking around. After about 20 minutes it started, nausea. It got worse and I thought I’d wasted it all, I didn’t want to do any training. I couldn’t imagine doing this for 5 more days. I got out of the lift and managed to get myself down into the landing field safely. I bundled up the glider and dropped it under some trees where other pilots were relaxing. I didn’t want to do anything other than sit. It was a 2 hour wait until I could think about packing my glider and even then I had to take my time.

As I finished I saw Joseph coming in along with Evo, the instructor from SkyNomad. I was able to say hello to him and that was about all. Joseph came over and I explained my situation. I wasn’t expecting this at all, I was used to going round and round but had never experienced anything like this. We talked it through, Joe explained that this often happened to pilots, mainly at the start of the season after a layoff, travel sickness tablets would help and he had some…..

Joseph and Tristan introduced me to modern technology and installed XCTrack on my phone and fitted this safely to my meagre flight deck, now I had 2 instruments, an old alt/vario and my phone.

So on Monday with some trepidation I joined the other students in the school room to introduce ourselves and understand the aim of the training and how we would go about it. We spent a lot of time looking at weather forecasts and I was beginning to understand how important this is for planning flying. Back in the day it was the BBC and if you could afford it Wendy Windblows, a series of independent weather stations on top of some local take offs, Wether Fell for example. A phone call to the station gave an indication of cloud cover, wind strength and gusts. It could all change by the time you got there but it was better than nothing!

After the intro it was decided to go up Sopot mountain, a 10 minute drive in the school bus and then a skylift to the top. The operatives were used to helping pilots with their gear on and off and there were no incidents. The ride up gave me time to take my Stugeron, I let them dissolve in my mouth for a more rapid response, goodness I hope these work. It wasn’t the only thing in my mouth, I am going to be properly flying!

My first flight was really uneventful, just a bit of lift to play with and the instructors were assessing us for competence too I’m sure. My track log didn’t work so I can’t count that one but a second flight the same day after 30 minutes in the air resulted in a 350m height gain and 15.7km, wow, I can do this and I wasn’t feeling ill.

Next day more of the same, 17 minutes just a top to bottom, 2km with 735m height gain. I was low over Sopot village and Nicky, the CFI was down with me too. On the radio I heard ‘there’s lift over the town’, what he meant was don’t go over the town! As the pilot in charge I thought ‘oh f..k, this is far too low’ and headed for the landing field, no time to do an approach, aim for the field, whack through a tree top and land downwind. Another of my nine lives used.

It’s Wednesday now and after a lot of looking at the weather forecasts it was back up Sopot again. Now it gets more complicated with the instructors flying with us as well taking off in between the students, so instructor, 2-3 students, instructor, 2-3 more students then the following instructor. The idea was that they would show us where the thermals were and if someone was failing one of them would spiral down to help locate more lift. There was a plan to fly west for about 5 km then use the higher northerly wind to cross the flat land to the ridge in the south, maybe 40km. There wasn’t a gaggle and we got spread out.

My best flight though, 121 minutes, 5.6m/s lift, touched cloud base at 3013m with 1841m height gain, XC Track says 75.1km. They gave us all trackers, I packed up and walked to the road and after 10 minutes my retrieval arrived, how about that!

https://www.xcontest.org/world/en/flights/detail:2wheelsbetter/9.7.2024/09:35

Other flights were from a site called Dobrostan, a heck of a drive but SkyNomad wanted us to fly. 2 on Thursday the first was poor, 6km and the second after 59 minutes 20.4km

Then Friday, the last day, 45 minutes and 20.3km, only modest lift, 551m height gained. Joseph and Tristan followed us to this site, they arranged transport with a local pilot who taxied them for a modest sum. After landing they were both quite near and they located me with Google Map Sharing and collected me which was good, thank you, it was a long drive in the bus.

We all had flights every day, Joseph and Tristan were able to enjoy their time there too,I’m sure they can bend your ear with some stories if you want them to. It was warm all the time, some days a bit overcast but generally better flying weather than we have here and SkyNomad ticked all the boxes as far as I’m concerned.

I’ve  been following the Telegram threads, one topic is whether to take an SIV course just post CP, some pilots say yes, some no. I guess I will need one if I am to continue but I’m pleased with my results from this week away, I learned a lot when we had theory while waiting for better conditions and have become much more familiar with my equipment as well. Now it’s the same for us all, waiting for the right day to get out, I think it may be a while before I can replicate some of these results. In the meantime I will be ticking off the tasks for my Pilot rating… mind though if you see a red and white Geo 3 behaving badly the chances it will be me and I am open to constructive criticism.

sections.title: From the Archive
sections.content: A report of Colin Rider’s epic climb to 13,600’ back in 1987.

sections.title: PG Tips 🫖 (Paragliding tips)
sections.content:
Stephen Craven reports

Hi all.

This month's tip:  attaching wing to harness with harness facing the wing.

I notice quite a few new faces on the hill so I think it’s worth going over some basics with the lines. I practise these things for myself on occasion otherwise I forget and then have difficulties on the hill when it’s windy and want to get flying and not waste time faffing with lines.

Step 1

Get your lines in order As on top.

Step 2

Hold the attaching part of the lines neatly and in order. Here I have the left side risers on top

Step 3

Turn risers anti clockwise.

Step 4

Complete 180 degree turn. Now the right hand risers are on top.

Step 5

The top riser controlling the right side of the wing goes to the left of the harness. Remember

after reverse launching you will twist and the lines will no longer cross.

Step 6

Make sure the carabiner is not twisted and faces outward and attach the risers squarely.

Step 7

The bottom riser controlling the left side of the wing can now be attached.

NB If you turn to the left after launching the wing then the left risers go on top as I have been describing.

But if you turn to the right after launching the wing the right risers need to go on top.

Step 8

All done and ready to launch

Happy flying! Hope to see you on a hill soon.
